name: All-or-Nothing Thinking
description: Seeing things in black-and-white categories with no middle ground.
examples: If I'm not perfect, I'm a total failure.
Either I do it right or not at all.
reframingQuestions: Is there a middle ground between these extremes?
Can you think of shades of gray in this situation?

name: Overgeneralization
description: Making broad conclusions based on a single event.
examples: I failed once, so I'll always fail.
Nobody ever listens to me.
reframingQuestions: Is this truly always the case?
Can you think of exceptions to this pattern?

name: Mental Filtering
description: Focusing exclusively on the negative details of a situation while ignoring the positive.
examples: My boss gave me a great review but mentioned one area for improvement, so I must be terrible at my job.
I ruined the whole party because I spilled my drink.
reframingQuestions: What positive details am I ignoring in this situation?
If a friend were looking at this, what good things would they notice?

name: Catastrophizing
description: Expecting the worst possible outcome without considering other possibilities.
examples: If I make a mistake, I'll definitely get fired.
This headache must be a brain tumor.
reframingQuestions: What's the most likely outcome?
How have similar situations turned out in the past?

name: Mind Reading
description: Assuming you know what others are thinking without evidence.
examples: They must think I'm incompetent.
She didn't smile because she hates me.
reframingQuestions: What evidence do you have for this assumption?
Could there be other explanations for their behavior?

name: Fortune Telling
description: Predicting that things will turn out badly without any realistic evidence.
examples: I just know I'm going to fail this exam.
There is no point in trying because they will reject me.
reframingQuestions: Can I really predict the future with 100% accuracy?
What is an alternative, more positive way this could play out?

name: Emotional Reasoning
description: Believing that because you feel a certain way, it must be the objective truth.
examples: I feel overwhelmed, so my problems must be impossible to solve.
I feel like a bad friend, so I must be one.
reframingQuestions: Am I confusing a feeling with a fact?
What objective evidence contradicts how I am feeling right now?

name: Should Statements
description: Using rigid rules about how you or others should behave.
examples: I should always be productive.
They should know how I feel.
reframingQuestions: Where does this rule come from?
What would happen if you replaced 'should' with 'prefer'?

name: Labeling
description: Assigning global, negative traits to yourself or others based on a single behavior.
examples: I made a mistake, therefore I am a loser.
He cut me off in traffic, he is a complete monster.
reframingQuestions: Is this label a fair summary of the whole person?
Can I describe the specific behavior instead of using a global label?

name: Personalization
description: Taking the blame for events that are not entirely under your control.
examples: It's my fault the project failed, even though the client changed the requirements.
My child got a bad grade, I must be a terrible parent.
reframingQuestions: What other factors contributed to this outcome?
Am I taking responsibility for someone else's choices or random circumstances?
